All right, the funnest topic of all time, but really it would be most necessary in this portal, which is giving and receiving feedback. Okay. Why is this so important? Because the fastest way for you to grow in your role as a leader is by growing those who report to you. Okay. So, the fastest way for you to grow as a leader is to grow those who report to you. What's the fastest way to grow somebody? Feedback. Okay. The fastest way to grow anyone on your team is to give them feedback. And so, if you think about it, it's like losing weight. If you want someone to, at the least, stop gaining weight, just have them weigh themselves daily. Right? Because then you're like, oh crap, I've been gaining weight. And then suddenly, like, they stop reaching for that extra cookie. Right? Obviously. So, what you need to understand, it's in our nature to respond faster to outside feedback than internal feedback. Okay. It's like, with us here at Gym Launch, people respond to fast and honest feedback, because they want fast and honest feedback, because we are supposed to be advising them on business decisions. And those are obviously very important. Right? So, they don't want sugarcoated crap. They want to hear the truth. And that will help them save money and learn faster. Right? And so, if you think about it, without timely and honest feedback, your growth is always going to be limited. Okay. And so, if you don't give timely and honest feedback to people on your team, then you are limiting their growth. Okay. What does timely mean and what does honest mean? Okay. Timely means immediately after the action. I'll go over that again. And honest means critical if need be. So, like we said before, a lot of people don't like to be confrontational, but they want to be honest. But most of the time, being honest means being kind of confrontational. So, just remember that. And also remembering that no matter how self-aware you think you are, you have blind spots, as do your supervisors and director boards. Okay. And so, there are three pieces to healthy feedback that I want to break down. Honest, timely, and helpful. Okay. To go into that, we have to understand how to receive feedback that is helpful. Okay. Because, actually, studies show that it's not as hard for people to give feedback as it is for people to receive feedback. And if there's anything wrong with your culture, it's typically not the giving of feedback that's going to be the problem. It's the receiving of it without being defensive. Okay. So, the number one thing that you need to do is break up with the idea that feedback is negative. Okay. So, think of the people that have helped you in life. Parents, teachers, etc. Okay. Were they focused on you succeeding or were they focused on you liking them? Okay. You do not want to dread feedback. You want to crave it because you should crave it because it's going to help you grow. Okay. It's like with us. If I don't tell you that you need to change things in your business, if I'm like, oh, you know what? I would rather that Jimona really like me. So, I'm not going to tell them that they're, you know, they're messing up with their team. Yeah. I'm just going to let them go out of business. Yeah. That's a good idea. You know, I don't care if somebody is going to like the feedback I have because my job is to make sure you're successful. Right. And so, I have to give you the unsugarcoated feedback, which might be something that could hurt your feelings. Right. Okay. Two is remembering that feedback is a do not a who. Okay. So what you need to break up with is the idea of that's just how I am. It's always been that way. I've never been good at this. Okay. And I hear that a lot when I'm talking to actually the y'all as gym owners as well as our team, which is just like, that's just who I am. This is the way it's always been. I've really never been good at that. And so that's identifying with an action. And as a human, you have, you don't, you are not what you do. Right. And so it's very important that when you're receiving feedback, you understand that it's an action. It's not who you are. Okay. And so it's just flipping that switch in your mind so that you're more receptive to what somebody has to say. And so also remembering that that is a fixed mindset. The truth is you have the ability to change your performance at any point in time, especially if it's just an action you're taking, you know, someone might say, I'm not organized. And actually saying you're not organized doesn't mean you're bad at organization. Saying you're not organized creates the belief in your mind that you're bad at organization, which then means that you're not even going to try. Okay. And so that's why it's a fixed mindset, not a growth mindset. And 99% of the time, the truth is, is that what triggers you to get the most offensive when receiving feedback is where you really need to lean in and listen. Because usually we have anger within ourselves about the fact that we have that as a deficit. Right. And then third is you want to ask clarifying questions. So if somebody's giving you feedback, you're getting it from the team, from customers, etc. You want to ask questions that lead to specific outcomes. So if you ask general questions, you're going to get general answers and general answers lead to general actions. Okay. So instead you want to be very specific. Can you give me examples? Can you explain to me exactly what you mean by this? And I have to do this constantly because most of the feedback I do get is general. Well, people aren't happy that we did this. I'm like, well, what the heck does that mean? You know, and then you dig in, you dig in. Okay, who's not happy? How many people are not happy? What did they say? Show me the conversations. And usually it's much of a different issue than it was when it was brought to you. Right. That's why it's important to ask clarifying questions. So again, can you give me an example? What specifically could I do better next time? What would ideal scenario look like going forward? And then how would you have liked me to handle this otherwise? Right. Part two is giving feedback. All right. So there are two key pieces to giving feedback. There's when and who. Okay. When do you give feedback? What I want to ingrain in your mind before we go further is that if the first time he gives on feedback is during a performance review, you are far too late. That is not the point of a performance review. Okay. There should never be anything unexpected on a performance review. Otherwise, you are seriously too late. And so you need to normalize giving feedback by giving it immediately and giving it often. Okay. And so before we know about when do you have feedback? What I want you to understand is when not to give feedback. Okay. Never deliver feedback right before they have to perform a certain job and they have a lot of pressure on it. Okay. That could mean that they're running a meeting. They have a presentation. They're about to go into a sales call. Anything of that matter, you do not deliver feedback right before. It does not matter how bad you feel and how you're like, I need to get this off my chest. Okay. Don't emotionally vomit all over people. You need to have control. Be like, okay. It's not a good time to get feedback. I'm going to have to give it a different time. And then second is not giving feedback in public when it has nothing to do with the rest of the team. That's really just oddballing someone out and putting them on the spot and leading out of fear rather than empowering people. Right. No, however, that public feedback is only acceptable if it is something that the rest of the team address the person as well. And it's the team who's holding the person accountable. So, for example, on a time when it would be appropriate, I'll give you an example is we had someone who asked the entire team to schedule a meeting. And there was, I think 11 of us at the meeting. And they said, I really can't make that time. Can we please reschedule to this day? And the whole team was like, all right, cool. And then that person did not come to the meeting. And so that would be a time to hold that person accountable and from the rest of the team, because if the team doesn't know that it's been addressed, the team will not respect you or the person. Number two is how, okay. First off, you want to explain before you deliver feedback what's not happening. Okay. So you want to disarm the person. You are not losing your job and you are not getting fired. You want to always start off with that or if it's the team you're talking to, it's like, we're not going out of business. I'm not firing everyone here. Okay. And so what that does, it allows the team member to feel a sense of safety, because you've eliminated their biggest fear because everyone when they have a one on one or they get, you know, put a meeting on their schedule, they're automatically going to be freaking out up until the meeting because they're like, what do you want to talk about? What do they want to talk about? Right. And so they're going to assume it's the worst, which is like, they're going to get fired and they're going to lose their job and then they're going to be freaked out during the entire time you're talking, waiting for you to drop the ball. Right. And so how do you actually deliver the feedback once you've gotten past these couple of things? Okay. One, you want to pick one to two areas of feedback you believe will bring the best return on investment. Okay. And then two, you want to tell them exactly what they can do to improve. So it doesn't matter if someone's done like 11 things wrong. Pick the two that are the biggest issues. The biggest issue because I'm guessing the other nine result from the top two. Right. And then you need to tell them exactly what they can do to improve, meaning you say this is what you did wrong. Here's how it looks when it's done right. Okay. In most cases, it is fit to explain that it rarely does a team member do something intentionally to harm the team. So, explain to them, I understand that your intention was to X, Y and Z, but I want to explain to you that the impact it had was actually negative. So I know that you went well. However, the outcome was not. Okay. And then lastly is how do you speak? This is incredibly underrated and should probably have an entire presentation of its own. But the fact is that most people hear your words, but they feel your attitude. And so you have to go into this meeting unemotional and come from a place of logic. And this requires emotional mastery over yourself. Because your team is smart. People are intuitive. They know how you are feeling. And so you have to always at all times show a great level of neutralness, neutrality, whatever is a word. So that your team knows and it doesn't scare them when they go into a meeting like this. They should just see that you're speaking as you would normally speak. The words are just different. I hope that makes sense. It's all about your tone. If you come across with huge inflectuations, you're clearly very stern. You come across annoyed. They're not going to take the feedback. Whereas you come across neutral, like you have your best interest and their best interest in mind and you're weighing the two and you're talking about both. People are going to be much more receptive to the feedback. Right. And then lastly is how to give feedback to your boss. And this is something I believe that every leader should be teaching their team. If you want to feel comfortable giving your boss feedback, then you need to ensure that you are doing the following so you have the right environment to give it. And this is something I have a lot of experience with because I get feedback quite often from people who report to me. And it's not always things I want to hear, but I can tell you when I am most receptive to it. Right. One, you need to remember that as your boss honors you, you must honor them. Okay. And so you need to be able to recognize your boss and appreciate them just like they appreciate you. So the reason I'm saying that is because think about like this. Do you accept criticism from people who never encourage you, who never tell you that they think well of you. They never tell you that they think that you were doing well at your job. Probably not. Two, when's the last time you compliment or should have graduated to your boss? Just think about it. Okay. Because any person of authority is more likely to be receptive to feedback if they've had an abundance of more positive interactions. So if prior to you delivering the feedback to them, you've established a good relationship and you've always expressed when you've been appreciative of them and all of that. Because humans are the same, whether it's the boss or the employee, like people feel the same. If you haven't told them enough that they're doing well, they don't want to be there to receive when you want to tell them what they're doing wrong. Okay. It's just the law of human nature. Two is to be prepared with solutions and suggestions to your critical feedback. Okay. So if you have feedback around a project, an interaction or decision, then you need to be prepared to tell them what you think could have been done better to make it better for the team. You can't come in just being like, you did this wrong. It's like, no, you have to have the two parts to it, which is like, here's where it didn't look good to the team. Here's where we haven't taken it well as a team. Here's where I think that you could have done this differently so that we could have taken it better. It would have been better for the team. It's the same. And then three, don't sugarcoat it. Be truthful. Why? Truth trumps flattery. And your boss may not be happy with your feedback, but they will be grateful for it later on. And why is this? As those in the highest positions in a company, in authority places, they often receive the least feedback and instead they get continuous flattery with no idea what they could do to improve. And so they're very appreciative and it's refreshing almost to get the feedback because they're like, thank goodness somebody told me, like, why don't more people tell me the truth? And so remember, like, your boss should be receiving feedback as well. And so again, how do you give it the same way? You pick one to two areas of feedback that you think will bring the most ROI. You tell them exactly what they can do to improve and you come with suggestions and then you do not act or speak out of emotion. If you are emotionally charged about a subject, you don't go into it. Okay. And so remembering that if you're talking to your boss about something and you are still visibly and physically upset over it, then that's not the right time to talk to them because your boss is not going to be receptive to your feedback if you're visibly emotional. Okay. And so some questions I want to leave you with to ask yourself really just reflective questions. One, has the feedback that you've received ever angered you? Two, do you feel like you give real and honest feedback to all your director ports? Do you feel like you truly are confronting the things you're doing wrong? Are you truly giving them, pointing out all the things they do wrong so that they can grow and become better? If not, you're bottle-capping both of you. And then three, how can we as a team normalize feedback more to the point where it becomes a habit? Okay. And something to ask yourself and to ask your team. So I really highly suggest that you actually ask yourself these things on a regular basis. And if you do it intentionally for a couple months, it will become natural to you and it will also instill it in your team. You could even ask these things on your weekly reports to the people who report to you. So I hope that was helpful. Feedback is honestly the catalyst for growth in your business. And so therefore it is incredibly important that you implement this.
